speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Tibor Eisen, E-I-S-E-N. Tibor goes by the name of Max. Max was born in Moldova, Slovakia on March 15th, 1929. My name is Dave Harris. And this interview is being conducted in Toronto, Ontario, Canada. And today's date is February 15th, 1995. Max, could you please tell me about your early life in Moldova?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my earliest recollection is-- I must have been age five-- my father taking me on his bicycle. I had a paper lunch bag in my hand, and I was really scared. He was taking me down to the Talmud Torah, which is the Hebrew school. This was my first venture out of the house. And it was a nice, sunny day. And I was-- when I arrived down at this Talmud Torah, which was next door to a synagogue. There was a small yard where the kids were playing. And they were playing ball. And some were-- different type of games. And I was handed over to the teacher. And I remember being very scared. And my father left me there. And I guess from that moment on-- moment on, I really didn't hit it off with this teacher. And here I was at the Talmud Torah, learning Hebrew. My problem was that whatever we were learning in Hebrew, we had to translate it into Yiddish. And I didn't understand any Yiddish whatsoever because we spoke Hungarian at home. In the area where we lived, everybody was Hungarian-speaking. So this was the first problem. And in fact, a year later, when I started public school, I have to start to learn Slovak, which I didn't understand either. So within a span of one year, I had to learn two new languages. And I guess I was a pretty confused kid. And so I'll tell you about my family. At that time, I was a only son. We lived in a quite a big house with my paternal grandparents-- my grandfather, grandmother-- and my uncle an aunt. And I was the only child at that time. And later on, I had two younger brothers. One was born in 1934, and I guess the other one was born in 1936. And I had a little sister, and she was born in 1943. I lived in this nice, big house with lots of space around. My grandfather had a lumber yard. We had quite a big yard, and we had a very big fruit garden with many fruit trees. And my grandfather was taking care of these trees. He was quite an expert with trees. And we had a vegetable garden.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and we also raised our own chickens and geese and ducks. And, you know, we were sort of a very self-contained family, just like many families in our little town of our size were, most of them. How many people were in Moldova?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were approximately-- as far as I can recollect, about 3,000 people in this little town. We were in the middle of a farming area, and there were many farmers that lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were Jews were in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were approximately 40 Jewish families in town. And when I think back, I realize how very well organized this Jewish community was. We had a synagogue, as I mentioned before. And we had a Talmud Torah, Hebrew school, even though it only had two rooms. But there was quite an emphasis put on Hebrew learning. You know, we all had to start to go to Hebrew school at age 5. And once I started public school, around-- public school finished about 4 o'clock in the afternoon. And within a space of half an hour, we had to be down at the Hebrew school till 7 o'clock every evening. And we went to Hebrew school on Sundays as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I just want to say, my grandfather had a lumberyard. And he was in this particular business with my uncle. And my father had a small distillery. And he had a place called The Cellar. And it was actually a cellar where you had to go down about 15 or so steps underground. And there was sort of like a pub, but he was only serving like hard spirits where the farmers used to come to drink. And we also used to make some liqueurs that he was selling in bottles.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I recollect filling bottles with different liqueurs-- chocolate liqueurs, creme de menthe, or pear liqueur, and putting on all these fancy labels, you know, from different parts of the world, and putting the red wax on the bottles to close the cork type of thing, and then put them in a straw and jacket. And I remember I used to deliver these to different customers. And I remember, my father had many of his friends come there, fellows his age. At that age, when I was five, six, seven, I thought that my father was a very old man. But I realize that he was only in his early 30s, actually. And so in this town, we lived with our neighbors who were Christians, Gentiles. You know, Jews did not live all together in one area. We were sort of dispersed in between. Most of the Jewish people were-- they had small businesses-- textile, yard goods, and, groceries, stuff like that. We were very Orthodox Jews, like most everybody in this town. All of the Jews were Orthodox Jews. And we went to synagogue on a regular basis. And especially on Shabbat, you know, Friday evening, we all went to synagogue with my grandfather, my father, and my uncle. And of course, Saturday morning again, we did that. And Saturday evening in the summer, we went again. In the winter, we usually stayed at home on Saturday evening, and we had our services, you know, between the three families-- my grandfather, uncle, and my father. And life was pretty good. I had many friends. We had a lot of fun. We had much space to play around in our own house, in the lumber shed, and we had an old stable. And we were playing, I remember, cowboys and bandits. We had all these books, you know, about cowboys and bandits. And we were not allowed to read these books, in fact. I remember I had to read these books sort of secretly because when my father found these books, he used to take them away from me and tear them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his objection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, his objection was that it was not the right thing to read. You know, I had to keep my mind on, on, on the learning. And I had a real problem, especially with the Hebrew school. The teacher wanted me to learn. He wanted to show my father that he's doing a real good job. And this teacher had a stick you know that he was-- he was really giving us the works with this stick. And I remember many a day that I was going home with a real bad-- he used to hit me, and I used to get a nose bleed, and my shirt was full of blood. And if I complained at home, I used to get some more from my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was the type of thing that went on. And I did not sort of [INAUDIBLE] to this particular type of thing. I remember many friends of mine that they were really fantastic in Hebrew learning. There were two of them, two brothers, the two Lichman brothers that are alive today-- one is living in Los Angeles, and one is living in Long Island, New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember so vividly that at the end of the [NON-ENGLISH], which is the end of the particular season, which was in the summer when our school year finished, the Hebrew school, and there were exams. And these two fellows and a few others, they were able to tell many pages of the Talmud, you know, without-- just by heart. I was always amazed at this. And so I was sort of learning all my years till about the age of 12. All this was sort of hammered in year after year, you know. It was like a, a repetition type of thing. And I really didn't understand much of what I was learning until I came back from the concentration camp, where I had to learn German. And then I understood sort of, you know, what I learned in the Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of subjects are you referring to? What type of understanding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Understanding, the [HEBREW], which is the five books of Moses-- Genesis, Exodus, and so on. This was the first thing that you started to learn in Hebrew school. And then there was the commentaries on that. And then we also learned [HEBREW], which is Talmud.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was-- life went on pretty good. I used to-- I remember we had-- my friends, as we were coming out of school every day, we used to go by the baker, whose name was Mr. Deutsch. I remember he was a tall man, and he always had a long pipe with a long stem, and he was always smoking this pipe. And we went into his bakery and picked it up little kaisers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came down to my father's cellar, and we used to take a little bite out of this kaiser, and go to these big drums full of liqueur, and open the spigot, and just drip some liqueur in. And everybody was having a good time. It was like a break before we went in to face our teacher in Hebrew school. So I remember these little kind of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sounds like you had a happy childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a very happy childhood other than-- the learning was a problem. Otherwise, everything was just great. And around 1936, I think, at that time, we knew about Hitler, who was in-- came into power in 1933. My father had a crystal radio. And somehow, they got, you know, that Hitler was making a speech which was, I guess, important for them to know about this speech, what he was saying. And several of his friends came to hear this speech. I did not understand any German, but I could hear this poison coming out of the radio. It was a screaming voice, you know. And all I can recall is the faces of these grownups. They were pretty shocked, you know, and they were talking between themselves. I didn't really pay much attention. But I thought it was some kind of a problem. And, you know, we really didn't know what the problem was going to be and until a few years after. And but life went on. I would like to say that-- I guess when I was about six years old, my paternal grandmother lived in about probably 100 kilometers from us in a little town called [NON-ENGLISH]. And they had a big farm. And my grandfather was not alive anymore. I don't remember my maternal grandfather. He must have died when I was a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my grandmother and her two sons-- my two uncles-- were running this farm. And these two uncles were not married. It was a big farm with many cattle. I remember they had a big herd of cows that the herdsmen took out every morning to the pasture. And they had many teams of horses because this is the way the fields were cultivated, with horses.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I loved to come here because when we got off the train, my uncle with a carriage, you know, with two beautiful horses, they were waiting for us. And I loved to drive the horses. And I was always sitting up in front with the driver. And I usually met two of my cousins, these two girls. They came from a neighboring town. And I only saw them every year in the summertime. And their name was Boiger. And I remember these two lovely girls. They were-- both of them were about a year or two older than I was. One was Edith and the other was Lily. And I was always looking forward to the summer to go to this place, to my grandmother's place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think I went there for about three or four years, right up until 1939. In, in '38, things started to change in Czechoslovakia. I became aware that something was not right because I would say in the summer of '38, my father and my uncle were called up to the army. And Czechoslovakia mobilized their forces. And I saw troops and tanks moving up to the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived near the Hungarian border. And I didn't know what was going on, but I know today that this was when part of Czechoslovakia was taken away, the Sudeten part, and was given to Nazi Germany. And several months later, which was March of 1939, Czechoslovakia ceased to exist. The Nazis invaded Bohemia, Moravia. And part of Slovakia became an autonomous country under Cardinal Tiso, who was a Nazi sympathizer. And this is where my maternal grandmother and my uncles and several uncles and cousins remained in Slovakia, and we became Hungarians. And so this was March, '39. And we were getting ourselves prepared for these Hungarian troops. I know that it was a fearful time because we were so familiar with the Czech troops. You know we lived right near the border. And many of these border guards, they used to come and drink in my father's place, and, you know, he was very friendly with everybody. I remember that he had a rule that whenever these border guards came to drink, they had to take their pistols out, and they had to put it in a [INAUDIBLE] in a drawer. I remember one day opening a drawer and it was full of pistols. You know, and as a child, I thought I'm going to play cowboys. And I had all my friends come down and have a look at this. You know, I was sort of showing off. And these border guards and the Czechs were-- they were like dressed, you know, properly, you know, and they were very well trained. And we had no-- I had no problem with the Hungarians coming in because I spoke Hungarian. I remember my grandfather, who was an officer in the cavalry in the First World War-- and he was in Franz Joseph's Army-- in fact, many people, including my grandfather, had a beard and these sideburns just like Franz Joseph had. And he was taking all his medals out that he received on this Russian front. He was there for over two years, I think. And he took this old uniform out. And this was the first time I saw this because under the Czechs, you know, you didn't want to show this off. So he was taking it all out. And he put this on. And this one afternoon, we were-- the whole townspeople-- all the townspeople were gathered in this central-- in the center of our town where the road was coming in from Hungary. And we were waiting for these Hungarian liberators, so-called. This is what they-- and many people in our town, all of a sudden, things have changed, you know. They-- under the Czechs, Jews were respected, you know. We had a democracy. And in Hungary, these things have changed. And we could see that people were changing their ways with us right away. There was a big gate put up. And there was a sign there, welcome Hungarian liberators. And I remember a couple of days prior to that, we were learning the Hungarian anthem, and we were all singing, you know, school kids. We were-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] oh. We were learning the anthem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Soon enough, we could see an officer on a horse leading a troop of soldiers. And when they came into town, and they were dragging themselves. And I remember as a child-- I was 10 years old, but I remember these patches on their pants. And they looked kind of bedraggled, you know. And I said, jeez, what kind of an army is this, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, my father put up a sign in his place of business. I'll call it the Cellar. This is what we called it in Hungarian, the [HUNGARIAN]. And he said, welcome, Hungarian liberators. And there were drinks served to these soldiers for two days-- gratis, you know, free drinks. And they were drinking away. I remember they were having a real good time. For two solid days, my father was handing out these drinks. And I remember we lived on the-- sort of at the edge of town near the railroad. Because lumber that was brought in from my grandfather, you know, it was close to the railway, so we lived there. And I had to come into town to school and  to my father's business, and I had to cross the railway-- the railroad. And there were the rail guards, you know, when, when a train comes, this post that comes down. The Hungarians put up guards there right away. For what reason, I really don't know what they were guarding. But instantly, as soon as he saw me coming, he said, you're a Jew. Because, you know, we as Jewish kids, we always wore a cap. So he could tell right away that I was a Jewish-- I was a Jew. So my father prepared little bottles with Schnapps, you know. And whenever I had to cross, I gave it to the guard, and he let me through without any problem. So we knew that changes were coming in Hungary. We were now Hungarians. We had a nanny. I guess she was like part of the family. She came to our house when I was born. And she practically raised the three of us kids. She knew all the Hebrew prayers. And Hungarian laws were-- started almost as soon as the Hungarians came in. And the first thing happened that-- within six months, my father's business was taken away from him. Jewish people could not sell any spirits. And his business was taken away without any compensation. And Jewish people could not sell any tobacco products. And Jewish people could not employ any Gentiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this nanny, she had to leave after 11 years of being-- living with us. And I remember we were very upset, and she was really upset. She had to go back to her home. And we had to hand in this crystal radio. We were not allowed to have a radio. And we had to have papers in our pockets when you went from one town to the next, this type of thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the whole atmosphere changed in the school. When we went to school, you know, there were fights with other kids, and Jewish kids had to sit in the back of the class. Our teachers changed. We had new teachers that came from Hungary. And I remember one particular teacher. He was quite cruel. We wore little side-- it's called payot, peyas, you know, like a sideburn-- not the one, you known that is curled, but just a small sideburn about an inch long. And he used to pick us up, Jewish kids, by the sideburn, and he said, just sit. And he used to pull us up from the chair. And sometimes, I thought that my scalp will come off. So these were these subtle things that were happening to us in school. And-- excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by 1941, the next things that came into-- the Hungarians brought in, they took away all the able-bodied young men from the age of 20 or 21 to 55 to forced labor camps. And my father and my uncle were taken away as well. And they were away for almost two years, on and off. They probably let them come home for a couple of weeks-- every six months, perhaps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in fact, in our town, they had to go to a central place where these groups were assembled away from our town. And in fact, in our town, we had a central where people came from other parts of Hungary to assemble themselves. And I remember that many hundreds of them were sleeping in our lumberyard, you know, just on straw, open to the elements. It had a cover on it, but the side was not covered in this lumber shed, you know. And it was in our yard where these people were living. And in fact, at night, some of them came into our house. And people were sleeping on the floor on, on straw mattresses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: These were Jewish people. These were all Jewish people that were taken to forced labor camp because Hungarians were taken into the army. You see, Hungary was an ally of Germany, so Hungarian troops were taken to the front line after 1941 when Russia was attacked. And Jews were taken to forced labor camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father was away for pretty well two years. I remember going to a synagogue this first year with my grandfather and with my two brothers. It was Rosh Hashanah, New Year's. And you know, when certain prayers are said, you know, and I remember my grandfather. He was kind of crying. He was trying not to show that he is crying, but his whole body was shaking. And I realized the seriousness, you know, because I looked around, and there wasn't any-- there weren't any young people in the synagogue. So life went on, you know. We really did not suffer hunger, you know, because we had our own food. We grew our food, vegetables and other things. And flour-- you know, we were able to buy flour to bake bread and stuff like that. So there was rationing, but we really didn't suffer any hunger.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember in 1941, my maternal grandmother and my uncles were taken away from Slovakia. And the way we found this out-- because their cattle herd was being brought to their grazing fields in Hungary because that land belonged to them, but the border happened to come right across the middle of this field.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the herdsman used to bring the herd of cattle over to the Hungarian side. And we had some relatives living in this town. And my grandmother used to send with the herdsmen once every now and then cheeses and butter and so on, and they used to hand it over to these relatives of ours.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day in 1941, they told these relatives, the herdsmen, that our-- that my grandmother and uncle were taken away. And I remember receiving-- we received a card from Lublin that they arrived there, and they are working. But these were cards, I know now, that the Germans had prearranged and to sort of quiet everybody's fears.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we realized that they were taken to Lublin, and we never heard from them ever again. That's my maternal grandmother, uncles, three aunts, and many, many cousins. I would say a total of about 40, extended family of about 40 people from my mother's side, you know, they just disappeared. There wasn't a single survivor. So 19-- by 1942, the next thing that the Hungarians did-- they were taking some Jewish people out of Hungary, shipping them out to Poland to occupy Poland, a place called [NON-ENGLISH]. They said that these are Hungarians-- these are Jews that don't have Hungarian citizenship.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was done, I know now, through the pressure from Germany, that they pressed the Hungarian government to deport some Jews. So they said, well, sort of a token number of people, about 35,000 Jews, were deported from Hungary in 1942. And this happened to myself. One morning, we were picked up by the gendarmes-- my mother, my aunt, myself, and my two younger brothers. And we were put on a train. It was-- these were cattle cars. And as we went along, we sort of-- we picked up people along the way at several stops. And we had two gendarmes inside the cattle cars with us. The doors were open. And it was like a friendly type of thing, you know. We thought we-- we really didn't think it was anything serious. They were just taking us somewhere, and everything is going to be fine. And eventually, we'll come home. This is what we thought. And so we were going from my place in Slovakia all the way up to the Carpathian Mountains. I remember we came to towns-- Satu Mare, which was previously Romania, but it became Hungary. And at every stop, the Jewish community came with food, and they brought us food at every stop. And they sort of advised the next stop that there is a transport coming with people. And they brought food to us. And so, you know, we didn't suffer any hunger. It was just a matter of not knowing when are we stopping and where we are going.
